name: Roswell Cover-Up
description: The Roswell Cover-Up theory centers on the alleged crash of an unidentified flying object (UFO) near Roswell, New Mexico, in July 1947. Proponents believe the U.S. military recovered not only wreckage of an alien spacecraft but also extraterrestrial bodies. The core claim is that the government has actively suppressed this information from the public for decades, engaging in a widespread conspiracy to conceal the truth about alien contact. The initial reports involved a crashed 'flying disc,' which the Roswell Army Air Field initially confirmed. However, this was quickly retracted, and the object was identified as a weather balloon. This retraction, coupled with witness accounts and alleged suppressed documentation, fueled the cover-up narrative. Evidence cited by proponents includes eyewitness testimonies of military personnel and civilians who claim to have seen debris and alien bodies, alleged leaked documents and memos hinting at a cover-up, and inconsistencies in the official explanations provided by the government over time. Mainstream explanations attribute the Roswell incident to the crash of a Project Mogul balloon, a top-secret program designed to detect Soviet nuclear tests. Skeptics argue that eyewitness accounts are unreliable due to memory distortion and suggestibility, and that alleged leaked documents are often forgeries or misinterpretations. The Air Force conducted investigations in the 1990s, concluding that the 'alien bodies' were likely crash test dummies used in high-altitude experiments and that recovered debris was from Project Mogul. Despite these official explanations, the Roswell incident remains a cornerstone of UFO lore and a symbol of government distrust for many.
subtitle: Alleged suppression of alien crash information

claims.claim: The U.S. military recovered a crashed alien spacecraft near Roswell in 1947.
claims.evidence: Eyewitness accounts of debris and alien bodies, alleged leaked documents.
claims.criticism: Official reports identify the object as a Project Mogul balloon; eyewitness accounts are often unreliable and subject to suggestion.

claims.claim: The government is actively concealing the truth about the Roswell incident from the public.
claims.evidence: Inconsistencies in official explanations, alleged classified documents, claims of witness intimidation.
claims.criticism: Government explanations have evolved over time as new information became available; alleged documents are often unverified or misinterpreted; witness intimidation claims lack concrete evidence.

claims.claim: Alien bodies were recovered from the crash site and are being studied in secret facilities.
claims.evidence: Eyewitness accounts, alleged photographs and videos, claims of reverse-engineering programs.
claims.criticism: No credible physical evidence of alien bodies has ever been presented; alleged photographs and videos are often debunked as hoaxes or misidentifications; reverse-engineering claims are unsubstantiated.

keyFigures.name: Jesse Marcel
keyFigures.role: Intelligence Officer involved in initial recovery efforts; later claimed it was alien debris.

keyFigures.name: Stanton Friedman
keyFigures.role: Ufologist and proponent of the Roswell cover-up theory.

keyFigures.name: Philip J. Klass
keyFigures.role: Skeptical investigator who debunked many Roswell claims.

timeline.event: Alleged UFO crash near Roswell, New Mexico.
Roswell Army Air Field announces recovery of a 'flying disc'.
Air Field retracts statement, identifies object as a weather balloon.
Eyewitness accounts and alleged documents emerge, fueling the cover-up theory.
U.S. Air Force releases reports attributing the incident to Project Mogul and crash test dummies.

sources.author: Philip J. Klass
sources.work: Roswell: UFO Crash or Weather Balloon?
sources.title: Philip J. Klass
